The ancient [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") was formed by members of this group, and today's Maya are generally descended from people who lived within that historical region. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Maya people](/w/index.php?title=Maya_people&redirect=no "Maya people"))

Indigenous people of Mesoamerica

This article is about the ethnic group. For the historical civilization, see [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization").

For other uses of "Mayan", see [Mayan (disambiguation)](/wiki/Mayan_(disambiguation) "Mayan (disambiguation)").

"Mayas" redirects here. For the Filipino ethnic group also known as "May-as", see [Sibuyanon](/wiki/Sibuyanon "Sibuyanon"). For the Cushitic ethnic group also known as "Maya", see [Maya (East Africa)](/wiki/Maya_(East_Africa) "Maya (East Africa)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Mayamap.png/250px-Mayamap.png)](/wiki/File:Mayamap.png)

The Maya area within [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica")

**Maya** ([/ˈmaɪə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MY-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Spanish: [[ˈmaʝa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) are an [ethnolinguistic group](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") of [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") of [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"). The ancient [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") was formed by members of this group, and today's Maya are generally descended from people who lived within that historical region. Today they inhabit southern [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), and westernmost [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), and [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras").

"Maya" is a modern collective term for the peoples of the region; however, the term was not historically used by the Indigenous populations themselves. There was no common sense of identity or political unity among the distinct populations, societies and ethnic groups because they each had their own particular traditions, cultures and historical identity.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

It is estimated that seven million Maya were living in this area at the start of the 21st century.[[9]](#cite_note-total-9)[[10]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-10) [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), southern [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") and the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula"), [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), and western [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), have managed to maintain numerous remnants of their ancient cultural heritage. Some are quite integrated into the majority [westernised](/wiki/Westernised "Westernised") *[mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo")* cultures of the nations in which they reside, while others continue a more traditional, culturally distinct life, often speaking one of the [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") as a primary [language](/wiki/Language "Language").

One of the largest groups of Maya, the [Yucatec Maya people](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya_people "Yucatec Maya people"), live in the Yucatan Peninsula, which includes the Mexican states of [Yucatán State](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_(state) "Yucatán (state)"), [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche "Campeche"), and [Quintana Roo](/wiki/Quintana_Roo "Quintana Roo") as well as the nation of [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize"). These people identify themselves as "Maya" with no further ethnic subdivision (unlike in the Highlands of Western Guatemala). They speak the language which anthropologists term "[Yucatec Maya](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya_language "Yucatec Maya language")", but is identified by speakers and Yucatecos simply as "Maya". Among Maya speakers, Spanish is commonly spoken as a second or first language.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

There is a significant amount of confusion as to the correct terminology to use – *Maya* or *Mayan* – and the meaning of these words with reference to contemporary or pre-Columbian peoples, to Maya peoples in different parts of Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, and to languages or peoples.

> *oxlahun ahau u katunil u 13 he›cob cah mayapan: maya uinic u kabaob: uaxac ahau paxci u cabobi: ca uecchahi ti peten tulacal: uac katuni paxciob ca haui u maya-bulub ahau u kaba u katunil hauci u maya kabaob maya uinicob: christiano u kabaob*   
> "Ahau was the katun when they founded the cah of Mayapan; they were [thus] called Maya men. In 8 Ahau their lands were destroyed and they were scattered throughout the peninsula. Six katun after they were destroyed they ceased to be called Maya; 11 Ahau was the name of the katun when the Maya men ceased to be called Maya [and] were called Christians."

[Chilam Balam](/wiki/Chilam_Balam "Chilam Balam") Chumayel[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERestall200467-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Mayanlanguageslocations.png/250px-Mayanlanguageslocations.png)](/wiki/File:Mayanlanguageslocations.png)

Map of Maya linguistic distribution

Linguists refer to the Maya language as Yucatec or Yucatec Maya to distinguish it from other [Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages"). This norm has often been misinterpreted to mean that the people are also called Yucatec Maya; that term refers to only the language, and the correct name for the people is simply Maya (not Mayans). (Yucatec) Maya is one language in the Mayan language family.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Confusion of the term Maya/Mayan as an ethnic label occurs because Maya women who use traditional dress identify by the ethnic term *mestiza* and not Maya.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Persons use a strategy of ethnic identification that Juan Castillo Cocom refers to as "ethnoexodus"—meaning that ethnic self-identification as Maya is quite variable, situational, and articulated not to processes of producing group identity, but of escaping from discriminatory processes of sociocultural marginalization.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The Yucatán's Indigenous population was first exposed to Europeans after a party of Spanish shipwreck survivors came ashore in 1511. One of the sailors, [Gonzalo Guerrero](/wiki/Gonzalo_Guerrero "Gonzalo Guerrero"), is reported to have taken up with a local woman and started a family; he became a war captain in the Postclassic Mayan state of [Chetumal](/wiki/Chetumal_Province "Chetumal Province"). Later Spanish expeditions to the region were led by [Córdoba](/wiki/Francisco_Hern%C3%A1ndez_de_C%C3%B3rdoba_(discoverer_of_Yucat%C3%A1n) "Francisco Hernández de Córdoba (discoverer of Yucatán)") in 1517, [Grijalva](/wiki/Juan_de_Grijalva "Juan de Grijalva") in 1518, and [Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") in 1519. From 1528 to 1540, several attempts by [Francisco Montejo](/wiki/Francisco_de_Montejo "Francisco de Montejo") to conquer the Yucatán failed. His son, Francisco de Montejo the Younger, fared almost as badly when he first took over: while invading Chichen Itza, he lost 150 men in a single day.[[17]](#cite_note-ambconq-17) European diseases, massive recruitment of native warriors from Campeche and Champoton, and internal hatred between the Xiu Maya and the lords of Cocom eventually turned the tide for Montejo the Younger. [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza") was conquered by 1570.[[17]](#cite_note-ambconq-17) In 1542, the western Yucatán Peninsula also surrendered to him.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Chichen_Itza_3.jpg/250px-Chichen_Itza_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chichen_Itza_3.jpg)

Chichen Itza's [*El Castillo*](/wiki/El_Castillo,_Chichen_Itza "El Castillo, Chichen Itza")

Historically, the population in the eastern half of the peninsula was less affected by and less integrated than the western half. In the 21st century in the Yucatán Peninsula (Mexican states of Campeche, Yucatán and Quintana Roo), has between 750,000 and 1,200,000 people speaking Mayan. However, three times more than that are of Maya origins, hold ancient Maya surnames, and do not speak Mayan languages as their first language.

[Matthew Restall](/wiki/Matthew_Restall "Matthew Restall"), in his book *The Maya Conquistador*,[[18]](#cite_note-18) mentions a series of letters sent to the King of Spain in the 16th and 17th centuries. The [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") Maya families at that time signed documents to the Spanish royal family; surnames mentioned in those letters are Pech, Camal, Xiu, Ucan, Canul, Cocom, and Tun, among others.

A large 19th-century revolt by the Maya people of Yucatán in Mexico, known as the [Caste War of Yucatán](/wiki/Caste_War_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Caste War of Yucatán"), was one of the most successful modern Indigenous revolts in Mexico.[[19]](#cite_note-19) For a period the Maya state of [Chan Santa Cruz](/wiki/Chan_Santa_Cruz "Chan Santa Cruz") was recognized as an independent nation by the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), particularly in terms of trading with British Honduras.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Dr_Luna_Kan_%282010%29.jpg/250px-Dr_Luna_Kan_%282010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr_Luna_Kan_(2010).jpg)

Former governor of Yucatán, [Francisco Luna Kan](/wiki/Francisco_Luna_Kan "Francisco Luna Kan"), was a Maya with the very common surname "Kan"

[Francisco Luna-Kan](/wiki/Francisco_Epigmenio_Luna_Kan "Francisco Epigmenio Luna Kan") was elected [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Governor of Yucatán") of the state of Yucatán from 1976 to 1982. Luna-Kan was born in [Mérida, Yucatán](/wiki/M%C3%A9rida,_Yucat%C3%A1n "Mérida, Yucatán"), and he was a doctor of medicine, then a professor of medicine before his political offices. He was first appointed as overseer of the state's rural medical system. He was the first governor of the modern [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula") to be of full Maya ancestry. In the early 21st century, dozens of politicians, including deputies, mayors and senators, are of full or mixed Maya heritage from the Yucatán Peninsula.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Poblado-maya-1.jpg/250px-Poblado-maya-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poblado-maya-1.jpg)

Yucatecen people

According to the National Institute of Geography and Informatics (Mexico's [INEGI](/wiki/INEGI "INEGI")), in Yucatán State there were 1.2 million Mayan speakers in 2009, representing just under 60% of the inhabitants.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Due to this, the cultural section of the government of [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_(state) "Yucatán (state)") began on-line classes for grammar and proper pronunciation of Maya.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Maya people from [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula") living in the United States of America have been organizing Maya language lessons and Maya cooking classes since 2003 in California and other states: clubs of [Yucatec Maya](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya "Yucatec Maya")[[22]](#cite_note-yucatecos-22) are registered in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") and [Irving, Texas](/wiki/Irving,_Texas "Irving, Texas"); [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") in Utah; [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"); and [California](/wiki/California "California"), with groups in San Francisco; San Rafael; Chino; Pasadena; Santa Ana; Garden Grove; Inglewood; Los Angeles; Thousand Oaks; Oxnard; San Fernando Valley and Whittier.[[22]](#cite_note-yucatecos-22) Maya language is taught at the college and graduate level; beginning, intermediate, and advanced courses in Maya have been taught at Indiana University since 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Open School of Ethnography and Anthropology offers immersion Maya courses in a six-week intensive summer program.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Chiapas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chiapas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Chiapas-Maya.gif/250px-Chiapas-Maya.gif)](/wiki/File:Chiapas-Maya.gif)

Maya populations in Chiapas. The area officially assigned to the [Lacandon Community](/wiki/Lacandon_Community "Lacandon Community") is the [Montes Azules Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Montes_Azules_Biosphere_Reserve "Montes Azules Biosphere Reserve"), which partly overlaps with the [Tzeltal](/wiki/Tzeltal_people "Tzeltal people"), [Tojolabal](/wiki/Tojolabal_people "Tojolabal people") and [Chʼol](/wiki/Ch%CA%BCol_people "Chʼol people") areas. Note. The [Zoque](/wiki/Zoque_people "Zoque people") are not Maya.

[Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas") was for many years one of the regions of Mexico that was least touched by the reforms of the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution"). The [Zapatista Army of National Liberation](/wiki/Zapatista_Army_of_National_Liberation "Zapatista Army of National Liberation"), launched a rebellion against the Mexican state, Chiapas in January 1994, declared itself to be an [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_the_Americas "Indigenous people of the Americas") movement and drew its strongest and earliest support from Chiapan Maya. Today its number of supporters is relevant. (see also the [EZLN](/wiki/EZLN "EZLN") and the [Chiapas conflict](/wiki/Chiapas_conflict "Chiapas conflict"))

Maya groups in Chiapas include the [Tzotzil](/wiki/Tzotzil_people "Tzotzil people") and [Tzeltal](/wiki/Tzeltal_people "Tzeltal people"), in the highlands of the state, the [Tojolabalis](/wiki/Tojolabal_people "Tojolabal people") concentrated in the lowlands around [Las Margaritas](/wiki/Las_Margaritas,_Chiapas "Las Margaritas, Chiapas"), the [Chʼol](/wiki/Ch%CA%BCol_people "Chʼol people") in the jungle, and in the south eastern [uplands](/wiki/Guatemalan_Highlands "Guatemalan Highlands"), the endangered [Mochó](/wiki/Motozintlecos "Motozintlecos") and the [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people"), also widely spoken in the [Guatemalan highlands](/wiki/Guatemalan_highlands "Guatemalan highlands"). (See map. Note. The [Zoque](/wiki/Zoque_people "Zoque people") are not Maya.)

The most traditional of Maya groups are the [Lacandon](/wiki/Lacandon_people "Lacandon people"), a small population avoiding contact with outsiders until the late 20th century by living in small groups in the [Lacandon Jungle](/wiki/Lacandon_Jungle "Lacandon Jungle"). These Lacandon Maya came from the Campeche/Petén area (north-east of [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas")) and moved into the Lacandon rain-forest at the end of the 18th century.

In the course of the 20th century, and increasingly in the 1950s and 1960s, other people (mainly the Maya and subsistence peasants from the highlands), also entered into the Lacandon region; initially encouraged by the government. This immigration led to land-related conflicts and an increasing pressure on the [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest"). To halt the migration, the government decided in 1971 to declare a large part of the forest (614,000 hectares, or 6140 km2) a protected area: the [Montes Azules Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Montes_Azules_Biosphere_Reserve "Montes Azules Biosphere Reserve"). They appointed only one small population group (the 66 Lacandon families) as tenants (thus creating the Lacandon Community), thereby displacing 2000 Tzeltal and Chʼol families from 26 communities, and leaving non-Lacandon communities dependent on the government for granting their rights to land. In the decades that followed the government carried out numerous programs to keep the problems in the region under control, using land distribution as a political tool; as a way of ensuring loyalty from different campesino groups. This strategy of [divide and rule](/wiki/Divide_and_rule "Divide and rule") led to great disaffection and tensions among population groups in the region.  
(see also the [Chiapas conflict](/wiki/Chiapas_conflict "Chiapas conflict") and the [Lacandon Jungle](/wiki/Lacandon_Jungle "Lacandon Jungle")).

### Tabasco

The Mexican state of [Tabasco](/wiki/Tabasco "Tabasco") is home to the [Chontal Maya](/wiki/Chontal_Maya_people "Chontal Maya people"). Tabasco is a Mexican state with a northern coastline fringing the Gulf of Mexico. In its capital, [Villahermosa](/wiki/Villahermosa "Villahermosa"), Parque Museo la Venta is known for its zoo and colossal stone sculptures dating to the [Olmec civilization](/wiki/Olmec_civilization "Olmec civilization"). The grand Museo de Historia de Tabasco chronicles the area from prehistoric times, while the Museo Regional de Antropología has exhibits on native Maya and Olmec civilizations.

## Guatemala

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Guatemala")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Jeunes_femmes_mayas.jpg/250px-Jeunes_femmes_mayas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeunes_femmes_mayas.jpg)

Young Mayan women in traditional dress in [Antigua, Guatemala](/wiki/Antigua,_Guatemala "Antigua, Guatemala")

In [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), Indigenous people of Maya descent comprise around 42% of the population.[[1]](#cite_note-census2018-1)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Many Maya still experience [discrimination and oppression](/wiki/Discrimination_against_Mayans_in_Guatemala "Discrimination against Mayans in Guatemala"). The largest Maya populations are found in the western highlands where they make up the majority of populations in the departments of [Baja Verapaz](/wiki/Baja_Verapaz "Baja Verapaz"), [Quiché](/wiki/Quich%C3%A9_Department "Quiché Department"), [Totonicapán](/wiki/Totonicap%C3%A1n_Department "Totonicapán Department"), [Huehuetenango](/wiki/Huehuetenango_Department "Huehuetenango Department"), [Quetzaltenango](/wiki/Quetzaltenango_Department "Quetzaltenango Department"), and [San Marcos](/wiki/San_Marcos_Department "San Marcos Department").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The Maya people of the Guatemala highlands include the [Achi](/wiki/Achi_people "Achi people"), [Akatek](/wiki/Akatek_people "Akatek people"), [Chuj](/wiki/Chuj_people "Chuj people"), [Ixil](/wiki/Ixil_people "Ixil people"), [Jakaltek](/wiki/Jakaltek_people "Jakaltek people"), [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people"), [Kʼicheʼ](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_people "Kʼicheʼ people"), [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people"), [Poqomam](/wiki/Poqomam_people "Poqomam people"), [Poqomchiʼ](/wiki/Poqomchi%CA%BC_people "Poqomchiʼ people"), [Qʼanjobʼal](/wiki/Q%CA%BCanjob%CA%BCal_people "Qʼanjobʼal people"), [Qʼeqchiʼ](/wiki/Q%CA%BCeqchi%CA%BC_people "Qʼeqchiʼ people"), [Tzʼutujil](/wiki/Tz%CA%BCutujil_people "Tzʼutujil people") and [Uspantek](/wiki/Uspantek_people "Uspantek people").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Qʼeqchiʼ live in lowland areas of Alta Vera Paz, Peten, and Western Belize. Over the course of the succeeding centuries a series of land displacements, re-settlements, persecutions and migrations resulted in a wider dispersal of Qʼeqchiʼ communities, into other regions of Guatemala (Izabal, Petén, El Quiché). They are the second-largest ethnic Maya group in Guatemala (after the Kʼicheʼ) and one of the largest and most widespread throughout Central America.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Guatemala_-_Madre_y_ni%C3%B1as_2007.jpg/250px-Guatemala_-_Madre_y_ni%C3%B1as_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guatemala_-_Madre_y_ni%C3%B1as_2007.jpg)

Mayans in Guatemala

In Guatemala, the Spanish colonial pattern of keeping the native population legally separate and subservient continued well into the 20th century.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
This resulted in many traditional customs being retained, as the only other option than traditional Maya life open to most Maya was entering the westeren culture at the very bottom rung. Because of this many Guatemalan Maya, especially women, continue to wear traditional clothing, that varies according to their specific local identity.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The southeastern region of Guatemala (bordering with [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras")) includes groups such as the [Chʼortiʼ](/wiki/Ch%CA%BCorti%CA%BC_people "Chʼortiʼ people"). The northern lowland Petén region includes the [Itza](/wiki/Itza_people "Itza people"), whose language is near extinction but whose agroforestry practices, including use of dietary and medicinal plants may still tell us much about pre-colonial management of the Maya lowlands.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

The Classic period of [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerican "Mesoamerican") civilization corresponds to the height of the [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"). It is represented by countless sites throughout Guatemala, although the largest concentration is in [Petén](/wiki/Pet%C3%A9n_Basin "Petén Basin"). This period is characterized by urbanisation, the emergence of independent [city-states](/wiki/Maya_city "Maya city"), and contact with other Mesoamerican cultures.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

This lasted until approximately 900 AD, when the [Classic Maya civilization collapsed](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse").[[31]](#cite_note-Gill_2000-31) The Maya abandoned many of the cities of the central lowlands or were killed by a drought-induced [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine").[[31]](#cite_note-Gill_2000-31)

### Genocide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Genocide")]

Main article: [Guatemalan genocide](/wiki/Guatemalan_genocide "Guatemalan genocide")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Rigoberta_Menchu_Tum.JPG/250px-Rigoberta_Menchu_Tum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rigoberta_Menchu_Tum.JPG)

Human rights activist and [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") laureate [Rigoberta Menchú Tum](/wiki/Rigoberta_Mench%C3%BA_Tum "Rigoberta Menchú Tum") in [Acaya](/wiki/Acaya "Acaya"), Province of Lecce (Italy), at the opening press conference of the International Forum of Peace.

The 36-year-long [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War") from 1960 to 1996 left more than 200,000 people dead, a half-million people driven from their homes, and at least 100,000 women raped; most of the victims were Maya.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The genocide against Mayan people took place throughout the whole civil war because Indigenous people were seen as supporting the leftist guerillas, but most acts against humanity occurred during [Efraín Ríos Montt](/wiki/Efra%C3%ADn_R%C3%ADos_Montt "Efraín Ríos Montt")'s presidency (1982–1983). Ríos Montt instituted a campaign of state terror intended to destroy the Mayas in the name of countering "communist subversion" and ridding the country of its Indigenous culture. This was also known as Operation Sofia. Within Operation Sofia, the military followed through with ["scorched earth policies"](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") which allowed them to destroy whole villages, including killing livestock, destroying cultural symbols, destroying crops, and murdering civilians.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In some areas, government forces killed about 40% of the total population; the campaign destroyed at least 626 Mayan villages.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

On January 26, 2012, former president Ríos Montt was formally indicted in Guatemala for overseeing the massacre of 1,771 civilians of the Ixil Maya group and appeared in court for genocide and crimes against humanity[[36]](#cite_note-36) for which he was then sentenced to 80 years in prison on May 10, 2013.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This ruling was overturned by the constitutional court on May 20, 2013, over alleged irregularities in the handling of the case.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The ex-president appeared in court again on January 5, 2015, amongst protest from his lawyers regarding his health conditions[[40]](#cite_note-40) and on August 25, 2015, it was deliberated that a re-trial of the 2013 proceedings could find Ríos Montt guilty or not, but that the sentence would be suspended.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Ríos Montt died on April 1, 2018, of a heart attack.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Guatemalan girls in their traditional clothing from the town of Santa Catarina Palopó on [Lake Atitlán](/wiki/Lake_Atitl%C3%A1n "Lake Atitlán")

The Maya people are known for their brightly colored, [yarn](/wiki/Yarn "Yarn")-based, [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles") that are woven into capes, shirts, blouses, [huipiles](/wiki/Huipil "Huipil") and dresses. Each village has its own distinctive pattern, making it possible to distinguish a person's home town. Women's clothing consists of a shirt and a long skirt.

The Maya religion is [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") combined with the Indigenous [Maya religion](/wiki/Maya_religion "Maya religion") to form the unique syncretic religion which prevailed throughout the country and still does in the rural regions. Beginning from negligible roots prior to 1960, however, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") has grown to become the predominant religion of [Guatemala City](/wiki/Guatemala_City "Guatemala City") and other urban centers, and mid-sized towns. The unique religion is reflected in the local saint, [Maximón](/wiki/Maxim%C3%B3n "Maximón"), who is associated with the subterranean force of masculine fertility and prostitution. Always depicted in black, he wears a black hat and sits on a chair, often with a cigar placed in his mouth and a gun in his hand, with offerings of tobacco, alcohol, and Coca-Cola at his feet. The locals know him as San Simon of Guatemala.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c2/Maximon%2C_in_Santiago_Atitlan.jpg/330px-Maximon%2C_in_Santiago_Atitlan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maximon,_in_Santiago_Atitlan.jpg)

Maximón, a Maya deity

The [Popol Vuh](/wiki/Popol_Vuh "Popol Vuh") is the most significant work of Guatemalan literature in the [Kʼicheʼ language](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_language "Kʼicheʼ language"), and one of the most important works of [Pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") American literature. It is a compendium of Maya stories and legends, aimed to preserve Maya traditions. The first known version of this text dates from the 16th century and is written in Quiché transcribed in Latin characters. It was translated into Spanish by the [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") priest [Francisco Ximénez](/wiki/Francisco_Xim%C3%A9nez "Francisco Ximénez") in the beginning of the 18th century. Due to its combination of historical, mythical, and religious elements, it has been called the Maya Bible. It is a vital document for understanding the culture of Pre-Columbian America. The [Rabinal Achí](/wiki/Rabinal_Ach%C3%AD "Rabinal Achí") is a dramatic work consisting of dance and text that is preserved as it was originally represented. It is thought to date from the 15th century and narrates the mythical and dynastic origins of the Toj Kʼicheʼ rulers of Rabinal, and their relationships with neighboring Kʼicheʼ of [Qʼumarkaj](/wiki/Q%CA%BCumarkaj "Qʼumarkaj").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Akkeren1999281,_288-44) The Rabinal Achí is performed during the Rabinal festival of January 25, the day of [Saint Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul "Saint Paul"). It was declared a masterpiece of oral tradition of humanity by UNESCO in 2005. The 16th century saw the first native-born Guatemalan writers that wrote in [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").

## Belize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Belize")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=). *(January 2012)* |

The Maya population in [Belize](/wiki/Belize "Belize") is concentrated in the [Corozal](/wiki/Corozal_District "Corozal District"), [Cayo](/wiki/Cayo_District "Cayo District"), [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo_District "Toledo District") and [Orange Walk](/wiki/Orange_Walk_District "Orange Walk District") districts, but are scattered throughout the country. The Maya are thought to have been in Belize and the Yucatán region since the second millennium BC. Much of Belize's original Maya population died as a result of new infectious diseases and conflicts between tribes and with Europeans. They are divided into the [Yucatec](/wiki/Yucatec_Maya_people "Yucatec Maya people"), [Kekchi](/wiki/Q%CA%BCeqchi%CA%BC_people "Qʼeqchiʼ people"), and [Mopan](/wiki/Mopan_people "Mopan people"). These three Maya groups now inhabit the country.

The Yucatec Maya lived in Northern Belize, and many more migrated from Yucatán, Mexico, to escape the Caste War (1847 to 1901). Several Yucatec Maya groups lived near the Yalbac area of Belize and in the Orange Walk district near the present-day Lamanai at the time of British contact. The Mopan are Indigenous to Belize but were forced out by the British; they returned from Guatemala to evade slavery in the 19th century. The Kekchi also fled from slavery in Guatemala in the 19th century. The latter groups are chiefly found in the Toledo District.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Maya cultural heritage tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maya_peoples&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Maya cultural heritage tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Tunkan_Maia_Yucatan.jpg/250px-Tunkan_Maia_Yucatan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tunkan_Maia_Yucatan.jpg)

Maya family from Yucatán



A boy playing Maya trumpet opposite of [Palacio Nacional, Mexico City](/wiki/National_Palace_(Mexico) "National Palace (Mexico)"), Mexico.

There is often a relationship between cultural heritage, tourism, and a national identity. In the case of the Maya, the many national identities have been constructed because of the growing demands placed on them by cultural tourism. By focusing on lifeways through costumes, rituals, diet, handicrafts, language, housing, or other features, the identity of the economy shifts from the sale of labor to that of the sale of culture.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Global tourism is now considered one of the largest scale movement of goods, services, and people in history and a significant catalyst for economic development and sociopolitical change.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Estimated that between 35 and 40 per cent of tourism today is represented by cultural tourism or heritage tourism, this alternative to mass tourism offers opportunities for place-based engagement that frames context for interaction by the lived space and everyday life of other peoples, as well as sites and objects of global historical significance.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In this production of tourism the use of historic symbols, signs, and topics form a new side that characterizes a nation and can play an active role in nation building.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

With this type of tourism, people argue that ethno-commerce may open unprecedented opportunities for creating value of various kinds. Tourists travel with cultural expectations, which has created a touristic experience sometimes faced with the need to invent traditions of artificial and contrived attractions, often developed at the expense of local tradition and meanings.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

An example of this can be seen in "Mayanizing Tourism on Roatan Island, Honduras: Archaeological Perspectives on Heritage, Development, and Indignity." Alejandro J. Figueroa et al., combine archaeological data and ethnographic insights to explore a highly contested tourism economy in their discussion of how places on Roatan Island, Honduras, have become increasingly "Mayanized" over the past decade. As tour operators and developers continue to invent an idealized Maya past for the island, non-Maya archaeological remains and cultural patrimony are constantly being threatened and destroyed. While heritage tourism provides economic opportunities for some, it can devalue contributions made by less familiar groups.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Films and television

* *[Kings of the Sun](/wiki/Kings_of_the_Sun "Kings of the Sun")* (1963), the first major motion picture that depicted a part of Maya history, in this case the conquest of [Chichen Itza](/wiki/Chichen_Itza "Chichen Itza") by [Hunac Ceel](/wiki/Hunac_Ceel "Hunac Ceel"), a famous Maya general.
* *[El Norte (film)](/wiki/El_Norte_(film) "El Norte (film)")* (1983), one of the first indie films ever produced, about two Maya siblings who immigrate illegally to the U.S. to escape the Maya genocide in Guatemala.
* *[Apocalypto](/wiki/Apocalypto "Apocalypto")* (2006), an adventure film directed and produced by Mel Gibson, set around the time of European contact with all of the dialogue spoken in [Yucatec](/wiki/Yucatec_Mayan_language "Yucatec Mayan language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* *[The Forgotten District](/wiki/The_Forgotten_District "The Forgotten District")* (2008), a documentary on Maya ecotourism in southern Belize.
* *[Heart of Sky, Heart of Earth](/wiki/Heart_of_Sky,_Heart_of_Earth "Heart of Sky, Heart of Earth")* (2011), a documentary on the Maya of today and their fight to save their culture and environment.
* *[Mayan Renaissance](/wiki/Mayan_Renaissance "Mayan Renaissance")* (2012), starring [Rigoberta Menchú](/wiki/Rigoberta_Mench%C3%BA "Rigoberta Menchú").
* *[Ixcanul](/wiki/Ixcanul "Ixcanul")* (2015), the first movie ever produced completely in [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_language "Kaqchikel language"), a Maya language. It's an indie film that relates the life of a young woman in a traditional Maya village.
* [*La Llorona*](/wiki/La_Llorona_(2019_film) "La Llorona (2019 film)") (2019), a horror film addressing the genocide of the Maya in Guatemala.
* *[Sons of Anarchy](/wiki/Sons_of_Anarchy "Sons of Anarchy")* and its sequel spin-off series, *[Mayans M.C.](/wiki/Mayans_M.C. "Mayans M.C.")*, both FX series, created by Kurt Sutter, feature a fictional Latino [outlaw motorcycle club](/wiki/Outlaw_motorcycle_club "Outlaw motorcycle club") that adopts much of the culture of Maya civilization, reflective in the club's name, the club's patch, and the terminology used by members.
* *[Black Panther: Wakanda Forever](/wiki/Black_Panther:_Wakanda_Forever "Black Panther: Wakanda Forever")* (2022), adventure film sequel with elements of the story of a superhuman Maya tribe with ancestors from the Mesoamerica jungle
